name: Dismal Swamp
short_description: BLM public land near Mountain Home offering agate and jasper collecting on gentle terrain at 6641 feet elevation. No permit required for recreational rockhounding.
description:
Dismal Swamp is a popular rockhounding destination on Idaho BLM land in Elmore County, situated at 6641 feet elevation in the foothills above Mountain Home. The site features gentle slopes ideal for hiking and scanning for semi-precious stones, primarily agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The area's geology reflects the Owyhee-Bruneau upland volcanic landscape, where ancient basaltic flows and rhyolitic deposits have been weathered and eroded to expose colorful nodules and fragments across the surface.

This location is accessible year-round but most productive during spring and early fall when the ground conditions are optimal for spotting specimens. The gentle terrain makes it an excellent beginner-friendly site that requires minimal technical climbing or scrambling. Rockhounders typically walk the washes and hillsides scanning for naturally weathered agate nodules that range from thumb-sized to fist-sized, often displaying banding, color variation, and chalcedony rinds.

The BLM manages this area under standard recreational collecting guidelines, making it an affordable option with no permit fees or daily rates. Maximum collection is 25 pounds per person per day for personal use only. The landscape is relatively open with scattered low vegetation typical of Idaho high-desert foothills, providing good visibility for spotting specimens across exposed earth and rock faces.
history: This region sits within the Owyhee-Bruneau volcanic plateau, formed during the Tertiary period when extensive basaltic and rhyolitic volcanism created the high-desert landscape. Weathering and erosion over millions of years exposed nodular agate deposits and jasper fragments throughout the surface, making the area particularly productive for collectors seeking colorful semi-precious stones.
tips: Search the washes and gullies where water erosion has naturally exposed agate nodules at the surface; look for specimens with opaque chalcedony rinds that stand out against tan soil. Bring a small chisel or rock pick to test hardness and crack open promising nodules along natural fracture lines. Early morning light helps spot the subtle color variations of agate; focus on areas where hillside runoff has concentrated lighter-colored stones. The gentle slopes allow you to cover significant ground; plan to walk multiple directions rather than staying in one spot.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6641 feet on gentle slopes with exposed volcanic soil and scattered low sagebrush. Rolling terrain with good footing on compacted earth and rock. Walking distance from parking varies depending on access point; typically 0.5 to 2 miles of walking to productive collecting areas. No major obstacles or steep drop-offs.
best_season: April - October. Late spring through early fall offers the most comfortable temperatures and best ground conditions for spotting specimens. Winter snow is possible above 6600 feet; summer afternoons can be hot.
written_directions: From Mountain Home, take US 20 east approximately 8 miles. Turn north on a BLM access road (look for signage or ask at Mountain Home BLM office for current access points into Dismal Swamp area). Road conditions vary seasonally; high-clearance or 4x4 recommended during wet seasons. GPS coordinates 43.729045, -115.366414 mark a general collection zone.
road_conditions: Dirt and gravel BLM access roads; washboard surface typical. Passenger vehicle acceptable in dry conditions; high-clearance recommended during wet spring.
parking_notes: Informal parking pullouts along BLM roads near collection areas. No developed lot or facilities. Space for 3-5 vehicles at typical pullouts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Specimens must be hand-collected using non-powered tools.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection allowed
No motorized excavation or heavy equipment
Hand-collection only using non-powered tools
nearest_services: Mountain Home, approximately 8-10 miles south. Gas, food, water, and supplies available in town.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. No wheelchair access to collection areas. Dirt and gravel roads require standard footwear with good traction. Open terrain with minimal obstacles.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Dismal Swamp in Idaho?
faq.answer: Dismal Swamp is known for agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. The agate nodules often display beautiful banding and color variation, ranging from translucent to opaque. Many collectors find thumb-sized to fist-sized agates along the washes and hillsides.

faq.question: Is a permit required for rockhounding at Dismal Swamp?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Dismal Swamp sits on Idaho BLM public land where recreational rockhounding is allowed under standard personal use guidelines. You can begin collecting immediately without any fees or permits.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding near Mountain Home at Dismal Swamp?
faq.answer: April through October provides the most comfortable conditions. Late spring and early fall offer ideal temperatures and clear skies for spotting specimens. Winter snow is possible at this 6641-foot elevation, and summer afternoons can be hot.

faq.question: How much agate and jasper can I collect per day at Dismal Swamp?
faq.answer: The daily collection limit on BLM land is 25 pounds per person for personal use. This is enforced under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). Commercial collection is not permitted.

faq.question: Is Dismal Swamp rockhounding good for beginners and families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes. The gentle rolling terrain and exposed specimens make it ideal for beginners and families. Children as young as 6 can participate safely on the easy slopes. No technical climbing required, and the open landscape makes it easy to supervise kids while they search for agate nodules.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Dismal Swamp?
faq.answer: Bring a small chisel or rock pick to test and crack open promising nodules. Wear sturdy hiking boots with good traction for the dirt and gravel terrain. Pack plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hat for the high-desert exposure. A small backpack helps carry specimens within your 25-lb daily limit.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Dismal Swamp on Idaho BLM land near Mountain Home. No permit required. Easy collecting for beginners and families.
